events.text: For a high-risk medication like insulin, check the policies where you work[br4.46][br4.541]to determine if you need confirmation of the amount before administering it.[br8.884][br9.306]I'll say in my experience this has prevented some errors. One time, I saw a student start to use the wrong syringe,[br16.371][br16.452]and they were about to administer 3 mL instead of 3 units of insulin.[br21.427][br21.508]Thankfully, I was there to prevent that error before it happened.[br25.032]

As I said earlier, it can be stressful to think about making errors. But the truth is, we all make them sometimes.[br8.548][br8.629]We're all on the same team working to keep our clients safe, no matter what happens.[br13.403]

In your own practice, use your clinical judgment skills to anticipate possible errors before they happen.[br7.267][br7.348]If you're not sure about something, ask. And if you do make an error, let your manager or supervisor know immediately.[br15.705][br15.786]Like I said, everyone is on the same team working to keep our clients safe. Thanks for talking with me today![br23.134]
